---
title: "Roadblocks for Relaxation"
format: html
---
# Roadblocks for Relaxation

Many people in recovery know relaxation is important but struggle to do it consistently. Common roadblocks include guilt ("I should be productive"), constant stimulation, perfectionism, and not knowing which method works for their body.

Relaxation skills are not passive; they are active tools for staying alcohol-free. Lower baseline stress improves decision-making and reduces urge intensity.

When stress remains chronically high, cue reactivity and emotional impulsivity increase. Building realistic relaxation routines helps lower baseline arousal and protects against stress-triggered lapse.

## Roadblock Audit
| My roadblock | How it increases risk | One practical workaround |
|---|---|---|
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |

::: {.callout-important}
### Practice plan

Work through this in real-life situations rather than only reading it.

Test one relaxation practice for 10 minutes daily for five days.

If something feels hard, simplify the step and repeat it consistently.
:::
